definition: Running a 6-7% of GDP deficit at sub-4% unemployment has a pre-2022 base rate of 0/74 annual observations (1947-2024). All three occurrences (2022-2024) are in the current cycle. This factual finding means every regression-based fiscal impact estimate is being applied out of sample, requiring wider uncertainty bounds on all fiscal claims.
evidence: Joint frequency analysis: deficit >5% GDP AND unemployment <4.5% = 3/77 total, 0/74 pre-2022
No peacetime precedent at current magnitude (6-7% deficit at 3.6-4.0% unemployment)
WWII deficits exceeded 10% but employment was mobilization-driven, not comparable
Zero out of 74 annual peacetime observations since WWII match this configuration
Structural primary deficit 3-4 standard deviations above post-WWII mean at comparable unemployment rates
Near-factual observation unchallenged in any debate
open_questions: How should uncertainty bounds be scaled for out-of-sample extrapolation?
Are the mechanisms understood even if the configuration is novel, or does novelty imply emergent behavior?
Does the absence of historical precedent make this configuration inherently unstable or could it represent a new stable equilibrium?
Is the configuration a result of structural changes (demographics, healthcare costs) or discretionary fiscal choices?
